response:
The `/decide` process ran to completion via the PM agent (advisor panel → verifier → red-team → PM decision), and the ADR is committed at `docs/decisions/0001-free-tier-daily-cap.md`. Here's the decision record:

---

# ADR 0001: Free-tier daily per-key request cap

**Status:** Accepted · **Date:** 2026-07-14 · **Decider:** PM (full authority)

## Decision
**Cap = 300 requests/day/key**, shipped with four load-bearing pieces, not the number alone:
1. **Blocking measurement gate** — before any hard enforcement, re-aggregate the existing 30-day logs *by key* (not just the daily totals in the CSV) for p50/p90/p99/max. Falls back to a one-week read-only observation window if per-key history isn't retained.
2. **Grandfather, don't clip** — any existing key already averaging >300/day gets grandfathered/contacted, never hard-blocked mid-workflow.
3. **48h new-key grace** — new signups get alert-only for 48 hours before hard enforcement, so launch-day evaluators aren't walled off mid-test.
4. **Ship the conversion funnel with the cap** — instrument `cap-hit → pricing view → Pro signup`, plus the per-key percentile dashboard. Without this, the whole premise stays unfalsifiable.

## Rationale
The CSV math checks out (mean 100.4 req/key/day, busiest day 106/key, nothing near 1,000) — but that's an *aggregate* mean across ~40 keys, and the CSV has no per-key column. A mean of ~100 is fully consistent with skew where a handful of keys already run 400–2,000/day and are invisible in the daily totals. So 300 is a **positioned hypothesis**, not "measured-safe headroom" — the measurement gate exists to convert that unknown into a fact before anyone gets clipped.

The cap's job is conversion, not cost control (free-tier compute is cheap; Pro is unmetered). It only works as a lever if it binds on users who could plausibly pay — roughly 3x the verified mean positions it at the power-user segment (~9,000 req/mo) rather than the median hobbyist (~3,000 req/mo) who'll never convert and whose goodwill still feeds the top of the funnel.

## What would change the number
- **>5% of active keys already exceed 300/day** on the per-key gate → grandfather them and reopen the number upward.
- **<5% of cap-hitters upgrade within 30 days** → the cap isn't a working lever; don't reflexively lower it — check whether the funnel drop-off is at cap-hit→pricing-view or pricing-view→signup first (a pricing problem, not a cap problem).
- **>30% of cap-hitters go dormant** within 30 days → the cap is repelling, not converting; raise it.
- **>20% of keys regularly exceed 200/day** → the median is drifting into the cap; reassess before it becomes the new default experience.
- **>15% conversion with low churn** → hold at 300; don't chase the number upward just because it's working — that reads as a bait-and-switch to existing users.
- Hard revisit regardless at **90 days post-Pro-launch**.

## Rejected alternatives
- **A (1,000/day, already built):** verified to never bind against real usage — costs nothing to ship but does zero conversion work; free substitutes for Pro indefinitely. The sunk-cost choice.
- **B (150/day):** only ~1.4x the busiest observed day — blocked on verification because it's a near-mean cap set from aggregate data with no visibility into per-key peaks; risks clipping legitimate heavy users at launch for no real cost savings.
- **Shadow-mode enforcement:** rejected because the needed data already exists in logs — user-facing alerts would bias the very distribution being measured and mask silent churn.
- **Letting the raw data pick the number:** rejected because the cap needs an intentional segment target; grandfathering handles the edge cases better than an open-ended data-derived threshold would.

---

One gap worth flagging: this is the first ADR in the repo — there was no existing `docs/decisions/` corpus or ADR template to conform to, so ADR 0001 was created fresh. If you have a canonical template elsewhere, point me at it and I'll reconcile.

title: Free-tier daily per-key request cap
status: Accepted
date: 2026-07-14
decider: PM
tags: [pricing, conversion, contradiction, checkable, unverifiable]
summary: Free-tier cap = 300 req/day/key, gated on measuring per-key distribution from existing logs before enforcing, with new-key grace and conversion instrumentation shipped alongside.

---

# ADR 0001: Free-tier daily per-key request cap

## Weakest assumption (most visible)
The whole decision rests on an unverified belief: hitting the cap correlates with
willingness to pay $12/mo — the cap CONVERTS, not CHURNS. Pro is not live; zero conversion
data exists. [unverifiable] REOPEN-IF: under 5% of cap-hitters upgrade within 30 days of
first cap-hit (measurable once the funnel below ships). If false, no number fixes it — the
bottleneck is Pro's value, not the ceiling.

## Assumptions (falsifiability)
- [checkable] Before enforcement, the by-key log re-aggregation shows fewer than 5% of active
  free keys already exceeding 300/day on their own peak days.
- [contradiction] Hard-enforcing new signups on day one contradicts the measure-first safety
  rule; resolved by the 48h new-key grace (step 3).
- [unverifiable] REOPEN-IF: under 5% of cap-hitters upgrade to Pro within 30 days of first
  cap-hit (measurable once the funnel ships).

## Decision
Free-tier cap = **300 requests/day/key**, with:
1. **Measurement gate (blocking):** before ANY hard enforcement, re-aggregate the existing
   30-day request logs *by key* for per-key p50/p90/p99/max — the same source that produced
   the CSV, not new collection. If per-key history isn't retained, fall back to a one-week
   server-side, read-only observation window (no user-facing alerts). [checkable] confirm
   per-key logs are retrievable before flipping enforcement.
2. **Grandfather, don't clip:** any existing legitimate key measured >300/day is grandfathered
   or contacted — never hard-blocked mid-workflow. The number stays 300; grandfathering is the
   safety valve.
3. **New-key grace:** new keys get 48h alert-only, then hard 300/day — so launch-day evaluators
   batch-testing the API aren't walled mid-evaluation.
4. **Ship with the cap:** the funnel `cap-hit -> pricing view -> Pro signup` (timestamped) and
   the per-key percentile dashboard. Without these the premise is unfalsifiable.

## Rationale
Math verified from the raw CSV: 30 days, 35–45 keys/day, 3,300–4,770 req/day, mean 100.4
req/key/day, busiest day 2026-06-19 = 4,770/45 = 106.0/key, no daily *average* above 106.
But the CSV has only aggregate columns; the per-key **maximum is unknowable** from it. A mean
of ~100 across ~40 keys is consistent with skew where a few keys already run 400–2,000/day
(an 80/20 shape puts the top ~9 keys at ~424/day on the busiest day — invisible in aggregates).
So 300 is NOT "measured-safe headroom"; it is a positioned hypothesis, and the gate (step 1)
turns the unknown into fact before it can hurt anyone.

Why 300: the cap's job is conversion, not cost (free-tier compute is noise; Pro is unmetered at
$12). A cap converts only if it binds on the segment that can pay — the power user running a real
pipeline (~9,000 req/mo at 300/day), not the ~3,000 req/mo hobbyist who won't pay and whose
goodwill feeds the funnel. 300 ≈ 3x the verified mean: the median stays free, power users cross
it and meet the upgrade prompt. The A/B/C tie-break is *which segment the lever bites* — 300
bites the convertible one.

Why measure-then-enforce, not shadow-then-guess: user-facing shadow alerts bias the distribution
they measure and let heavy users churn silently; the per-key data already exists in logs, so read
it directly and reserve grace for new-key onboarding only. Why the new-key rule is fixed here:
hard-enforcing new signups on day one exempted the highest-intent cohort (launch-day evaluators)
from the safety mechanism, on the day a solo founder has least slack — a **[contradiction]**
resolved by step 3.

## Revisit triggers (else hard revisit 90 days post-Pro-launch)
- **Distribution:** gate shows >5% of active keys already >300/day on peak days -> grandfather +
  reopen the number.
- **Conversion floor:** <5% of cap-hitters upgrade within 30 days -> cap isn't the lever; if
  cap-hit->pricing-view is high but pricing-view->signup low, it's a pricing problem — don't lower
  the cap.
- **Churn:** >30% of cap-hitters go dormant within 30 days -> cap repels; raise it.
- **Median drift:** >20% of keys regularly exceed 200/day -> median growing into cap; reassess.
- **Strong lever:** >15% cap-hitter conversion with low churn -> HOLD at 300 (don't chase upward;
  lowering later reads as a takeaway).

## Rejected
- **A — 1,000/day (built):** verified to never bind on observed usage -> zero conversion work;
  free substitutes for Pro forever. The sunk-cost number.
- **B — 150/day:** ~1.4x the busiest-day average (106); the verifier BLOCKED a near-mean cap on
  aggregate-only evidence — clips unknown heavy keys at launch and bites the non-converting median
  -> churn, no cost saving. A later target at most.
- **Shadow-mode for existing keys:** data is already in logs; alerts bias measurement and mask
  churn. Replaced by reading logs; grace kept only for new keys.
- **Let data pick the number:** the lever needs an intentional segment target; grandfathering
  guards the edges better than an open number.

Dependency: metering (Redis counters, alerts, dashboard) is per the memo — no source in repo to
cite, treated as claimed-but-unverified; confirm an alert-only per-key mode exists for step 3.
